Carballo :  M atrix New World Engineering,   Land Surveying and Landscape Architecture,   PC   (Matrix) is   pleased to  submit the following   prop osal   to   the Township of West Orange (Client).   This proposal encompasses  professional   site remediation   services   as requested for the above - referenced   property located ( the   Site).  The proposed Scope of Services described below are based on the NJDEP requirements set forth in the  Heating Oil Tank System Remediation Rules (N.J.A.C. 7:26F), Administrative Requirements for the  Remediation of Contaminated Sites (ARRCS; N.J.A.C. 7:26C), t he Technical Requirements for Site  Remediation (TRSR; N.J.A.C. 7:26E), NJDEP   Field Sampling Procedures Manual   (FSPM,   November  2022   edition), and applicable NJDEP guidance documents.  INTRODUCTION  D uring a   2017   geophysical investigation   at the Site a previously unidentified underground storage tank  (UST) was identified . The UST is located in the south - central portion of the lot and the anomaly identified  during the geophysical investigation was measured to be approximately 6 - feet by 10 - feet in size, which  indicates a potential 1,000 - gallon UST. The size, structure, and   contents of the UST were not confirmed  and there is no available documentation as the UST does not appear to be registered with NJDEP . Based  on the historical use of the site as a residence, the UST is assumed to have been used for heating  purposes. A review of historical aerial photographs indicates a structure has not been present on the  parcel since at least 1974. No staining was obs erved in the immediate area where the UST was identified.  In April 2017 soil samples collected around the perimeter of the UST did not identify detections of  Extractable Petroleum Hydrocarbons (EPH) . However, as the UST is not currently in - use, the tank ne eds  to be closed in accordance with NJDEP requirements.  In addition to the UST, the Site is located in area that is identified by the NJDEP as mapped historic fill.  A review of available historical Sanborn ®   Maps showed that entirety of the Site was identified as Marsh  Land. The potential presence of historic fill at the Site warrants further investigation.

SCOPE OF SERVICES  The scope   of services and associated fee is   described in detail below:  ITEM 1:   UHOT   CLOSURE ACTIVIT IES  Matrix will provide oversight activities of the   unregulated heating oil tank (UHOT)   removal and closure  activities. For estimation purposes, it is assumed the closure activities will be completed in   two   (2) days.  Matrix will retain a licensed tank removal contractor to properly decommission the   UHOT   in accordance  with applicable regulations and NJDEP guidance documents. The UHOT will be uncovered, cleaned,  removed, and set on poly sheeting for inspection by local official. Once inspection has been conducted,  the UHOT will be properly disposed. For   purposes of this   proposal   and based on the previous sampling  results, it is   assume d   no impacted soil will be removed   for off - site disposal. The UST excavation   will be  backfilled with clean overburden and approximately   15   tons of imported   certified clean fill   backfill material.  Costs are also included for disposal of up to   50 0 gallons of oil and residuals by vacuum truck.  Upon removal of the tank, Matrix will collect post - excavation soil samples for   laboratory analysis,  described below. If no groundwater is encountered, soil samples will be collected, every 5 feet, along the  centerline of the tank invert   (two samples) .   In addition, four sidewall samples will be collected from the  excavation and placed on hold, pending the results of the centerline samples.   If groundwater is  encountered within the excavation,   only the sidewall samples will be collected and will be automatically  analyzed .  All soil samples will be submitted for laboratory analysis for Category   2   extractable petroleum EPH with  contingent analysis for   polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHS) on 25% of those samples were EPH is  detected at a concentration above 100 milligrams per kilogram (mg/kg) , pursuant to N.J.A.C. 7:26E - 2.1.  For the purposes of this proposal , it is assumed that up to   six (6 ) soil samples will be submitted for total  EPH analysis and up to   two (2)   soil samples will be further analyzed for   PAHs .  All samples will be submitted for analysis to a NJDEP - certified laboratory on a standard turn - around time.  Note:   Based on the findings of the UHOT removals, further investigation and/or remediation may be  warranted. This proposal does not include costs associated with additional investigation/remediation  services.  Estimated Cost (Time and Material) Item 1   $ 1 4 , 7 00.00  ITEM 2:   HISTORIC FILL EVALUATION  In order to evaluate the potential presence of historic fill material at the Site,   Matrix will install two test  pits within the suspected historic fill area to a depth of two feet below the fill material to determine the  vertical and general horizontal extent of the fill.   Matrix   will c ollect two soil   samples (one from each test  pit) , in accordance with the NJDEP Historic Fill Material Technical Guidance document dated April 2013.  One sample will be submitted for Target Analyte List (TAL) Metals and PAHs.   The second sample will  be analyzed for EPH and the full Target Compound List/Target Analyte List (TCL/TAL) analytical suite.  For the purposes of this   proposal,   it is assumed the historic fill samples will be collected during the UST  closure activities.  Estimated Cost (Time and Material)   Item   2 :   $ 3 , 4 00 .00

ITEM   3 :   REPORTING  Matrix will prepare a Remedial Action Report (RAR) in accordance with N.J.A.C. 7:26E - 5.7.   The report  will document the field activities, results, findings and conclusions of the UHOT closure activities. The  report will include laboratory reports, analytical summary tables, and maps that document the sample  locations and results. Based on the fi ndings of the remedial activities, recommendations will be provided  for additional investigation and/or remedial action, if warranted. Matrix will also prepare the r equired  UHOT   System Remediation Form. On behalf of   Client , Matrix will submit the UHOT form, RAR, and  required $400 fee to the NJDEP.  A separate summary report will be prepared for client detailing the findings from the historic fill evaluation.  The summary letter will include data summary tables, a map depicting the sample locations, analytical  summary tables, and recommendations to add ress the presence of historic fill at the Site, if necessary.  Estimated Cost (Time and Material)   Item   3 :   $1 4 , 6 00.00  Total Estimated Cost:   $ 3 3 , 7 00 .00  CLOSING  Any item listed above which is not ordered will not be billed. Separate authorization from the client will  be requested prior to commencing services outside the scope of this   proposal.  All reimbursable   expenses including, but not limited to, application fees, laboratory testing costs, mylar  copies, certified mailings, photographs, blueprints, and special deliveries are considered additional to the  proposal items unless specifically noted within the scop e of this proposal.  The terms and conditions of this proposal are subject to the attache d Matrix   Terms and Conditions for  Professional Services . A current   Fee Schedule is also attached.  This proposal is submitted solely and exclusively for the use of   Ports America   for consideration of   the  professional services of Matrix .   Disclosure of this proposal’s content to any third party without prio r  written authorization from Matrix   is expressly prohibited.  In addition to the specific items as listed herein, the client may be required to demonstrate compliance  with certain permit and approval conditions as may be imposed by one or more of the regulatory agencies.  These   conditions may require   revisions   to the   plans   and/or   preparation   of   additional   supporting  documentation. This proposal does not include these additional items unless specifically outlined within  the scope of this proposal.  PAYMENT SCHEDULE  Payment shall be in accordance with the Charges, Billing, and Payment s chedule outlined in the Terms  and   Conditions attached to this proposal unless prior written arra ngements have been made with Matrix .  Please indicate your acceptance of this proposal by signing in the space provided below and returning  one copy to this office.   Acceptance of this proposal signifies the clients’ understanding that Matrix will  not be retained or asked to perform any servic es unless funding is secured and is available to pay all  invoices within   thirty ( 30 )   days.   Receipt of the signed proposal shall be considered authorization to  proceed with   all items described within this agreement.   Any items not intended to be authorized   shall be  clearly and specifically noted as such   within the client's signed and returned proposal.

Matrix New World Engineering , PC  26 Columbia Turnpike  Florham Park, NJ 07932  P:   973.240 - 1800   F:   973.240.1818  www.mnwe.com  F: \ 2025 \ 25 - 0037 West Orange DPW \ Proposal \ Hazmat \ Limited Hazmat Surv ey and demo specs propos al   -   25 Lakeside _D PW_FINAL.docx  1  March 3 , 20 2 5  Ms. Zayibeth Carballo &   Mr.   James Abbott  Township of West Orange  66 Main Street  West Orange, NJ 07052  Re:   PROPOSAL FOR   HAZARDOUS BUILDING MATERIAL   SURVEY AND TESTING ,  BUILDING MATERIALS ENVIRONMENTAL SAMPLING,  AND   CONTRACT DOCUMENTS AND BID SUPPORT  THE   DEPARTMENT OF PUBL I C WORKS   (DPW)  2 5 LAKESIDE AVENUE  WEST ORANGE , NEW JERSEY  MATRIX NO.   2 5 - 0037  Ms. Carballo &   Mr.   Abbott ,  Matrix New World Engineering, Land Surveying and Landscape Architecture, PC   (Matrix) is pleased to  present   the   Township of West Orange (Client)   this proposal to   conduct a comprehensive regulated  building material (RBM) investigation and sampling/testing program for asbestos - containing materials  (ACMs) as well as “other” potentially hazardous materials and/or conditions, including universal, and/or  regulated wastes   within the areas that will be impacted as part of this demolition/redevelopment project.  It   includes the DPW Maintenance/Office Building, Animal Control Building, Salt Shed and other ancillary  structure s.   Costs are also included in this proposal to provid e bid specifications and documents and bid  assistance for the demolition of the DPW Site.  Finally, it is Matrix’s current understanding that the building materials (i.e. concrete, brick, cinder block)  will   be   disposed of off - site as construction and demolition (C&D) waste. This disposal method will require  sampling of the building materials per the disposal facilities requirements. The building materials pre -  demolition   sampling   for the potential to reuse on - s ite or dispose of the materials at a Class B recycling  facility. Matrix   has provided   costs for each disposal   method,   below.  SCOPE OF WORK  Based upon the information provided, it is understood that the two - story, 49,800 square foot DPW  building and the 1,800 square foot Animal Control Building located at 25 Lakeside Avenue, New Jersey  (Site)   are   proposed for demolition. The proposed RBM work will be completed in accordance with all  applicable Federal, State, and local requirements. Matrix will perform the work consistent with the Site  Remediation Reform Act (SRRA), N.J.S.A. 58:10C - 1 er seq., the   Administrative Requirements for the  Remediation   of Contaminated   Sites   (ARRCS), N.J.A.C. 7:26C,   Technical Requirements   for Site  Remediation (TRSR), N.J.A.C. 7:26E, New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection (NJDEP),  NJDEP Fill Material Guidance for SRP Sites (October 2021, Version 4.0), NJDEP Fiel d Sampling  Procedures Manual, and any other applicable NJDEP Guidance Documents.  Matrix proposes to conduct the following activities summarized below.  TASK 1   –   ABESTOS - CONTAINING MATERIAL BUILDING SURVEY  Asbestos - Containing Material Survey  As part of the   Hazardous Building Materials Survey , Matrix will complete a comprehensive asbestos  survey of the site structure ( s ) . The asbestos sampling program will be performed by   Matrix personnel  who hold all required   Environmental Protection Agency ( EPA ) / Asbestos Hazard Emergency Response

F: \ 2025 \ 25 - 0037 West Orange DPW \ Proposal \ Hazmat \ Limited Hazmat Surv ey and demo specs propos al   -   25 Lakeside _D PW_FINAL.docx  2  Act ( AHERA )   certifications and are   accredited Asbestos Inspectors. Prior to the survey, Matrix will review  readily available previous asbestos survey reports, as - built drawings, and other available construction  documents for the property. Because the structure   is   planned for demolition, a more detailed survey,  including intrusive sampling, will be required   and   compared to a building survey for a limited renovation  project. This may include drilling through floors, structural slabs, exterior walls, and found ation walls to  access materials typically not inspected during limited surveys for renovation projects. All roofing systems  will be cored for sampling and repaired with roofing cement and fiberglass fabric. Please be advised that  this process will void any   existing roof warrantees and will be conducted only with the permission of the  Client. It is assumed that   all   roof s   are   accessible and in safe condition to perform sampling. If it is deemed  by Matrix’s field personnel that the roof is unsafe for sampling, roofing materials may be assumed ACM  until further safety precautions ( i.e.,   an ariel lift for access and to allow for safe   fall protection   procedures)  can be   provided .  The asbestos   survey will include the collection of bulk asbestos   samples of suspect ACM within  accessible areas that may have the potential for impact during demolition activities. All samples will be  analyzed for asbestos content using   p olarized   l ight   m icroscopy (PLM). Non - friable organically bound  (NOB) materials that are determined to contain less than one percent asbestos through PLM analysis,  such as caulks, glazing, mastics, roofing materials, and roof flashing, will be analyzed for asbestos  content   u sing   t ransmission   e lectron   m icroscopy (TEM). All samples will be analyzed by an independent  laboratory certified by the American Industrial Hygiene Association (AIHA) and the National Voluntary  Laboratory Accreditation Program (NVLAP). Based on our understanding of the proposed scope   of work  (demolition) and only upon receiving authorization from   the Client , a limited amount of destructive  sampling will occur during our survey. Suspect material observed or presumed to exist within inaccessible  areas will be   documented, characterized (if applicable), quantified and assumed to contain asbestos. For  the purposes of this proposal, a maximum of 1 0 0 PLM (friable), 1 3 6   PLM - NOB (non - friable) and 1 3 6   TEM  samples will be collected for analysis   utilizing a 1 - week turnaround time . If additional sample analysis is  required, we will require written authorization, or the material will be assumed to contain asbestos.  Other Hazardous Materials  In addition to   the   asbestos survey, the existing buildings and affected areas of the site will be investigated  to determine if equipment, transformers, or light fixture ballasts contain   polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs) .  This will be accomplished by reading equipment tags and contacting manufacturers for information on  their construction. Many transformers and fixtures are clearly identified whether they contain PCBs either  directly or based on the product serial number   or model number. In addition to PCB containing equipment ,  other hazardous materials such as mercury - containing equipment, fluorescent light bulbs,   refrigerants,  and other stored hazardous materials will be surveyed. An inventory of PCBs and other hazardous  materials will be prepared based on the findings of the investigation.   It is assumed that painted building  materials will be assumed lead - containing and all construction/demolition activities will follow all OSHA  guidelines as well as other lead safe work practices to be outline d   in the project specifications.  TASK 2   –   ABESTOS - CONTAINING MATERIALS SURVEY FINDINGS   SUMMARY  The results of the   Hazardous Building Materials Survey   will be summarized in a   H azardous Building  Materials Survey Report. This   r eport will discuss the survey methodologies, the sampling locations and  results, and will identify hazardous materials detected in the building structures and affected areas of the  site. The   r eport will summarize all work accomplished, and will include (i) all laboratory data, (ii) sketch  plans identifying the location of all samples obtained, (iii) summary tables identifying all analytical results,  including, but not limited to, detection and   friability levels of all ACM/non - ACM, and inventory of other  haz ardous materials.  TASK   3   –   HAZARDOUS MATERIALS CONSTRUCTION DOCUMENTS  Matrix will provide a certified Asbestos Project Designer to review the findings presented within each  Matrix Hazardous Building Materials Survey Report and provide on - site verification and delineation for

F: \ 2025 \ 25 - 0037 West Orange DPW \ Proposal \ Hazmat \ Limited Hazmat Surv ey and demo specs propos al   -   25 Lakeside _D PW_FINAL.docx  3  the ACMs identified; including multi - layered flooring systems and thermal system pipe insulation located  within wall and ceiling cavities.  Matrix will prepare the required specifications and bid sheets for necessary asbestos   abatement  associated with the anticipated structure demolition activities. The construction documents will include  specifications in Construction Specifications Institute   (CSI) format. Construction documents will include  requirements for asbestos abatement (removal), waste management, and additional health and safety  procedures in accordance with New Jersey Department of Labor (NJDOL) and Occupational Safety and  Health Adm inistration (OSHA) requirements. Our costs assume the submission of abatement documents  at the 100% Issue for Bid/Permit Phase only. Design drawings will not be included as part of Matrix’s  construction documents.  TASK 4   –   CHARACTERIZATION OF BUILDING MATERIALS (I.E. CONCRETE AND CINDER  BLOCK)  It is Matrix’s understanding that the building materials (i.e. concrete, brick, cinder block) will be disposed  of off - site as C&D waste. If this disposal method (C&D waste) is selected, then the sampling associated  with option 1 below will be required. If   the Township/redeveloper determines that they would like to re -  use the materials on - site as sub - base under asphalt paved areas or dispose of the material at a Class B  recycling facility, then the sampling associated with option 2 below will be required.   If   option 2 is   the  preferred disposal option, then the building material sampling is   recommended to be completed in - situ  (prior to building demolition)   as per   NJDEP   recommendations , however this option   does not guarantee  the material can be re - used on - s ite .   T he qualification for on - site re - use will   depend on the analytical  results of the building material   sampling . Costs for both options are   summarized   below.  OPTION 1  If option 1 is selected, Matrix will collect the concrete samples for disposal after the building is demolished.  Matrix will collect up four (4) ,   5 - point composite building material (concrete and cinder/concrete block)  samples (one composite sample per every 1,000   cubic yards [ CYs ] ) for waste characterization purposes,  as required for off - site disposal. Samples will be submitted to a New Jersey certified laboratory for  analysis of   e xtractable petroleum hydrocarbons category 2 (EPH Category 2), target compou nd list/target  analyte list plus a forward library search (TCL/TAL+30), full toxicity characteristic leaching procedure  (TCLP), TCLP beryllium,   Resource Conservation and Recovery Act ( RCRA )   characteristics, paint filter,  and contingent hexavalent chromium analysis. Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) will be collected as  a discrete grab sample and not a composite sample. If total chromium is over   20   milligrams per kilogram  ( mg/kg ) ,   contingent   hexavalent chromium will be   analyzed .   The samples will be analyzed   on a standard  14 - day   turnaround time. Upon receipt/evaluation of analytical results, Matrix will submit the required  documentation to the receiving facility in order to have the material approved for transportation and  disposal.  OPTION 2  Based on previous site and remedial investigation activities and Site Plans, the structure measures  approximately 49,800 square feet with a floor thickness of 6 - inches.  As part of this scope, Matrix proposes   the   collect ion of   approximately 20 building material samples   (15  samples from the DPW Building and   five   [5]   samples from the Animal Control Building) .   Twelve   ( 1 2 )  concrete chip samples are proposed to be collected from the building slab s (nine samples from DPW  Building and t hree   samples from   Animal   Control Building) . Matrix proposes   the   collect ion of   up to   nine  ( 9 ) cinder /concrete   block samples   (six samples from the DPW Building and two samples from the Animal  Control Building) . The total number of samples collected from each building material type is based on  measurements collected by Matrix   and building plans   provided by the Township   to determine the  approximate volume of each material present in the existing building. All samples will be   discrete surficial  samples not to exceed 1 - inch in depth and will be   biased to   any evidence of   impacts (i.e., staining) .   A ll

Based on historic soil and concrete analytical  results, the concrete chip samples will be submitted to a New Jersey certified laboratory for TCL/TAL+30  and EPH category 2   analysis . Contingent   samples for   hexavalent chromium   analysis   will be collected  and run if total chromium exceeds 20   mg/kg. In addition,   sufficient sample volume will be collected in  order to run   contingent synthetic precipitation leaching procedure (SPLP) analysis should concentratio ns  exceed the NJDEP migration to groundwater exposure pathway soil remediation standard   (MGWSRS) ,  in an attempt to establish a site - specific migration to groundwater exposure pathway soil remediation  standard. All samples will be submitted on a standard turnaround time. The analytical data package for  all samples will be a NJDEP Reduced Deliverables   package. The sampling is expected to be completed  in two days.  All analytical results for concrete will be   compared to the NJDEP MGWSRS, the Non - Residential  Ingestion - Dermal   Exposure   Pathway   Soil   Remediation   Standards   (NRIDSRS),   Non - Residential  Inhalation   Exposure Pathway Soil Remediation Standards (NRISRS), Residential   Ingestion - Dermal  Exposure Pathway Soil Re mediation Standards (RIDSRS), and the Residential Inhalation Exposure  Pathway Soil Remediation Standards (RISRS).  TASK 5   –   CONCRETE LETTER REPORT  Upon completion of the concrete sampling activities, Matrix will summarize the concrete data in table  format and the results will be compared to the NJDEP’s soil remediation standards. The letter report will  include a site plan depicting the sample locations, analytical summary tables, figures with analytical  results summary, and preliminary recommendation s. Matrix can also provide an associated cost estimate  and estimated timeline for beneficial reuse evaluation or disposal of the concrete , at additional cost .  TASK 6   –   CONTRACT DOCUMENTS AND BID SUPPORT  Matrix will complete a site   inspection   concurrently with the building material sampling   and prepare project  specifications for the building removal. Matrix will coordinate with all utility companies, as required,  regarding the shutoff of the existing utilities.  It is anticipated that local Soil Conservation District approval will be required for this project. Matrix will  prepare the application forms and plans for certification as part of this task; however, this proposal does  not include any application fees. Th e project specifications will include information on soil erosion  requirements. The final site will include topsoil and seed restoration.  The specifications will be prepared in accordance with the public bidding format. Once specifications are  complete Matrix will coordinate with the Township and provide assistance   throughout the bidding  process. This will include the advertisement of the bi d, response to all bidder questions and the review  of the bids, and preparation of a recommendation of award to the Township.  TASK 7   –   PROJECT MANAGEMENT  This task includes project management ,   and ,   the anticipation of future conference calls and/or meetings  with the project team. As a part of this task, the Matrix LSRP will oversee the activities outlined above.  COSTS  All work will be completed in accordance with our terms and conditions (attached). 103-25  March 25, 2025  A RESOLUTION   APPROVING   A   PROPOSAL FROM MATRIX NEW WORLD   FOR  ENVIRONMENTAL SERVICES   AT   5 5   LAKESIDE   AVENUE  (FORMER BARTON PRESS SITE)   NTE $ 104,715 .00  WHEREAS,   on   March   3 ,   202 5 ,   the   Township   of   West   Orange   (the   “ Township ” )  received   a   proposal   from   Matrix   New   World   Engineering,   Land   Surveying,   and   Landscape  Architecture,   P.C.   ( “ Matrix ” )   to   perform   environmental   services   at :   5 5   Lakeside   Avenue   ( Former  Barton   Press ) ,   West   Orange,   New   Jersey,   also   known   as   Block   1 15   Lot s   15   &   17   on   the  Township of West Orange Tax Map;   and  WHEREAS,   the   former   Barton   Press   is   part   of   the   Lakeside   Avenue   Brownfield  Development   Area   (BDA)   as   identified   by   the   New   Jersey   Departm ent   of   Environmental  Protection (NJDEP) Office of Brownfield and Community Revitalization ;   and  WHEREAS,   the   work   will   consist   of   Matrix   conducting   a   comprehensive   regulated  material   (RBM)   investigation,   sampling/testing   for   asbestos   -containing   material   (ACMs),  reporting and preparation of specifications and bid documents;   and  NOW, BE IT HEREBY RESOLVED BY THE TOWNSHIP COUNCIL OF THE  TOWNSHIP   OF   WEST   ORANGE ,   that   the   Township   accepts   the   proposal   from   Matrix  attached herein as Exhibit   “ A , ”   not to exceed   $ 104,715 .00   to complete the scope of work ;  AND   FURTHER   RESOLVED,   that   a   copy   of   this   Resolution   shall   be   made   available   in  the Clerk ’ s office in accordance with applicable law. Matrix New World Engineering , PC  26 Columbia Turnpike  Florham Park, NJ 07932  P:   973.240 - 1800   F:   973.240.1818  www.mnwe.com  1  March 5 , 2025  Ms. Zayibeth Carballo   &   Mr.   James Abbott  Township of West Orange  66 Main Street  West Orange, NJ 07052  Re:   PROPOSAL FOR   HAZARDOUS MATERIAL S   SURVEY AND TESTING ,  BUILDING MATERIALS ENVIRONMENTAL SAMPLING,  AND CONTRACT DOCUMENTS AND BID SUPPORT  THE FORMER BARTON PRESS BUILDING  55 LAKESIDE AVENUE  WEST ORANGE , NEW JERSEY  MATRIX NO.   2 1 - 1041  Ms. Carballo & Mr. Abbott ,  Matrix New World Engineering, Land Surveying and Landscape Architecture, PC   (Matrix) is pleased to present  the   Township of West Orange (Client)   this proposal   to conduct a comprehensive regulated building material  (RBM) investigation and sampling/testing program for asbestos - containing materials (ACMs) as well as “other”  potentially hazardous materials and/or conditions, including universal, and/or regulated was tes within the  areas that will be impacted as part of this demolition/redevelopment project.   C osts are also included in this  proposal to provide bid specifications and documents and bid assistance for the demolition of the   Former  Barton Press   Site.  Finally, it is Matrix’s understanding that the building materials (i.e., concrete, brick, cinder block) will be  disposed of off - site as construction and demolition (C&D) waste. This disposal method will require sampling  of the building materials per the di sposal facilities requirements. Matrix has provided two options for the  sampling of the building materials below, sampling pre - demolition for the potential to reuse on Site or disposal  of the materials at a Class B recycling facility and waste classificati on sampling for C&D disposal. Matrix  supplies costs for each disposal route below.  SCOPE OF WORK  Based upon the information provided, it is understood that the former two - story,   160,1 00 square foot   Barton  Press   Building located at   55 Lakeside Avenue , New Jersey (Site) is proposed for demolition. The proposed  RBM work will be completed in accordance with all applicable Federal, State, and local requirements.   Matrix  will perform the work consistent with the Site Remediation Reform Act (SRRA), N.J.S.A. 58:10C - 1 er seq., the  Administrative Requirements for the Remediation of Contaminated Sites (ARRCS), N.J .A.C. 7:26C, Technical  Requirements for Site Remediation (TRSR), N.J.A.C. 7:26E, New Jersey Department of Environmental  Protection (NJDEP), NJDEP Fill Material Guidance for SRP Sites ( October 2021 , Version   4 .0), NJDEP Field  Sampling Procedures Manual, and any other applicable NJDEP Guidance Documents.  Matrix proposes to conduct the following activities summarized below.  TASK 1   –   ABESTOS - CONTAINING MATERIAL BUILDING SURVEY  Asbestos - Containing Material Survey  As part of the   Hazardous Building Materials Survey , Matrix will complete a comprehensive asbestos survey  of the site structure ( s ) . The asbestos sampling program will be performed by   the Environmental   Protection  Agency ( EPA ) / Asbestos Hazard Emergency Response Act ( AHERA )   accredited Asbestos Inspectors .   Prior to  the survey, Matrix will review readily available previous asbestos survey reports, as - built drawings, and other  available construction documents for the property. Because the structure   is   planned for demolition, a   more  detailed survey, including intrusive sampling, will be required a nd   compared to a building survey for a limited  renovation project. This may include drilling through floors, structural slabs, exterior walls, and foundation

2  walls to access materials typically not inspected during limited surveys for renovation projects. All roofing  systems will be cored for sampling and repaired with roofing cement and fiberglass fabric. Please be advised  that this process will void any exist ing roof warrantees and will be conducted only with the permission of the  Client. It is assumed that the roof is accessible and in safe condition to perform sampling. If it is deemed by  Matrix’s field personnel that the roof is unsafe for sampling, roofing   materials may be assumed ACM until  further safety precautions ( i.e.,   an ariel lift for access and to allow for safe   fall protection   procedures) can be  provided .  The asbestos survey will include the collection of bulk asbestos samples of suspect ACM within accessible  areas that may have the potential for impact during demolition activities. All samples will be analyzed for  asbestos content using   p olarized   l ight   m icroscopy (PLM). Non - friable organically bound (NOB) materials that  are determined to contain less than one percent asbestos through PLM analysis, such as caulks, glazing,  mastics, roofing materials, and roof flashing, will be analyzed for asbestos content   u sing   t ransmission   e lectron  m icroscopy (TEM). All samples will be analyzed by an independent laboratory certified by the American  Industrial Hygiene Association (AIHA) and the National Voluntary Laboratory Accreditation Program (NVLAP).  Based on our understanding of the proposed scope   of work (demolition) and only upon receiving authorization  from   the Client , a limited amount of destructive sampling will occur during our survey. Suspect material  observed or presumed to exist within inaccessible areas will be   documented, characterized (if applicable),  quantified and assumed to contain asbestos. For the purposes of this proposal, a maximum of 1 3 0 PLM  (friable), 1 6 0 PLM - NOB (non - friable) and 1 6 0 TEM samples will be collected for analysis   utilizing a 1 - week  turnaround time . If additional sample analysis is required, we will require written authorization, or the material  will be   assumed to contain asbestos.  Other Hazardous Materials  In addition to   the   asbestos survey, the existing buildings and affected areas of the site will be investigated to  determine if equipment, transformers, or light fixture ballasts contain PCBs. This will be accomplished by  reading equipment tags and contacting manufacturers f or information on their construction. Many transformers  and fixtures are clearly identified whether they contain   polychlorinated biphenyl s ( PCBs )   either directly or  based on the product serial number or model number. In addition to PCB containing equipment , other  hazardous materials such as mercury - containing equipment, fluorescent light bulbs,   refrigerants,   and other  stored hazardous materials will be surveyed. An inventory of PCBs and other hazardous materials will be  prepared based on the findings of the investigation.   It is assumed that painted building materials will be  assumed lead - containing ,   and all construction/demolition activities will follow all OSHA guidelines as well as  other lead safe work practices to be outline d   in the project specifications.  TASK 2   –   ABESTOS - CONTAINING MATERIALS SURVEY FINDINGS   SUMMARY  The results of the   Hazardous Building Materials Survey   will be summarized in a   H azardous Building Materials  Survey Report. This   r eport will discuss the survey methodologies, the sampling locations and results, and will  identify hazardous materials detected in the building structures and affected areas of the site. The   r eport will  summarize all work accomplished, and will include (i) all laboratory data, (ii) sketch plans identifying the  location of all samples obtained, (iii) summary tables identifying all   analytical results, including, but not limited  to, detection and friability levels of all ACM/non - ACM, and inventory of other hazardous materials.  TASK   3   –   HAZARDOUS MATERIALS CONSTRUCTION DOCUMENTS  Matrix will provide a certified Asbestos Project Designer to review the findings presented within each Matrix  Hazardous Building Materials Survey Report and provide on - site verification and delineation for the ACMs  identified; including multi - layered floor ing systems and thermal system pipe insulation located within wall and  ceiling cavities.  Matrix will prepare the required specifications and bid sheets for necessary asbestos abatement associated  with the anticipated structure demolition activities. The construction documents will include specifications in  Construction Specifications Institute   (CSI) format. Construction documents will include requirements for  asbestos   abatement   (removal),   waste   management,   and   additional   health   and   safety   procedures   in  accordance   with   New   Jersey   Department   of   Labor   (NJDOL)   and   Occupational   Safety   and   Health  Adm inistration (OSHA) requirements. Our costs assume the submission of abatement documents at the 100%

3  Issue for Bid/Permit Phase only . Design drawings will not be included as apart of Matrix’s construction  documents.  TASK   4   –   CHARACTERIZATION OF BUILDING MATERIALS   (I.E. CONCRETE AND CINDER BLOCK)  It is Matrix’s understanding that the building materials ( i.e.,   concrete, brick ,   and cinder block ) will   be disposed  of off - site as C&D waste. If this disposal method   (C&D waste)   is selected, then the sampling   associated   with  O ption 1 below will be required. If the Township/redeveloper   determines   that they would like to re - use the  materials on - site as sub - base under asphalt paved areas or dispose of the material at a Class B recycling  facility , then the sampling associated with   O ption 2 below ,   will be required.   If   O ption 2   is   the preferred disposal  option, then the building material sam pling   is recommended   to be   completed in - situ (prior to building  demolition)   by the NJDEP , however this option   does not guarantee the material can be re - used on Site . The  qualification for on - site reuse will   depend on the analytical results of the building materials.   Costs for both  options are   summarized   below.  OPTION 1  If   O ption 1 is selected, Matrix will collect the concrete samples for disposal after the building is demolished.  Matrix will collect   up to   eight   ( 8 )   5 - point composite   building   material (concrete and cinder block)   samples  (one composite sample per every 1,000   cubic yards   [ CYs ] )   for waste characterization purposes, as  required for   off - site   disposal .   Please note   the total number of proposed samples is based on an estimation  of   the cinder block   present   in the interior portion of the building   (as it   has not   been measured due to mold  in that portion of the building)   and the area of   concrete   slab that is 12 - inches thick.   S ample s   will be  submitted to a New Jersey certified laboratory   and   analyzed for   Extractable petroleum hydrocarbons  category 2 (EPH Category 2), target   c ompound   l ist /target analyte list plus a   forward library search  (TCL /TAL+30) ,   full   toxicity   characteristic   leaching   procedure   ( TCLP ) ,   TCLP   beryllium,   Resource  Conservation and Recovery Act ( RCRA )   characteristics, paint filter, and contingent hexavalent chromium  analysis.   Volatile organic compounds (VOCs)   will be collected as a discrete grab sample and not a  composite sample. If total chromium is over   20   milligrams per kilogram ( mg/kg ) , hexavalent chromium  will be run.   The samples will be analyzed on a standard   14 - day   turnaround. Upon receipt/evaluation of  analytical results, Matrix will submit the required documentation to the receiving facility in order to have  the material appro ved for transportation and disposal.  OPTION 2  Based on previous site and remedial investigation activities, the structure measures approximately   580   feet  by   320   feet with a floor thickness of 6 - inches. During prior interior drilling activities, one area in the central  portion of the building near the bay door had a floor thickness of approximately   12 - inches (approximately   4 0  ft by   4 0   ft area). The interior   of the building in the office area and between the office area   and   the warehouse  area consist s   of cinder block . E xterior   portions   of the building co nsist ing   of cinder block   include   the bay doors  only .   The remainder of the building exterior consists solely of   corrugated metal .   The amount of cinder block in  the interior portion of the building has been estimated for this proposal.  As part of this scope, Matrix proposes   the   collect ion of   approximately 20 building material samples.   Fifteen  ( 15 ) concrete chip samples are proposed to be collected from the building slab. From the   interior   building walls  and loading dock wall , Matrix proposes to collect up to   five   ( 5 ) cinder block samples. The total number of  samples collected from each building material type is based on measurements   and/or estimations (interior  cinder block and area of the slab that is 12 - inchs think was estimated)   collec ted by Matrix to determine the  approximate volume of each material present in the existing building. All samples will be   discrete surficial  samples not to exceed 1 - inch in depth and will be   biased to any evidence of   impacts ( i.e.,   staining ) . The  samples will be field screened for VOCs using a photoionization detector (PID) and all field observations will  be documented in a field book. Based on historic soil and concrete analytical results, the concrete chip  samples will be submitted to a New   Jersey certifie d laboratory for TCL/TAL+30 and EPH category 2   analysis .  Contingent hexavalent chromium will be collected and run if total chromium exceeds 20 mg/kg. In addition,  sufficient sample volume will be collected in order to run   contingent synthetic precipitation leaching procedure  (SPLP) analysis should concentrations exceed the NJDEP migration to groundwater exposure pathway soil  remediation standard   (MGWSRS) , in an attempt to establish a site - specific migration to groundwater exposure

4  pathway soil remediation standard. All samples will be submitted on a standard turnaround time. The analytical  data package for all samples will be a NJDEP Reduced Deliverables package. The sampling is expected to  be completed in   two   day s .  All analytical results for   concrete will be compared to the NJDEP MGWSRS, the Non - Residential Ingestion -  Dermal Exposure Pathway Soil Remediation Standards (NRIDSRS), Non - Residential Inhalation Exposure  Pathway Soil Remediation Standards (NRISRS), Residential Ingestion - Dermal Exposur e Pathway Soil  Remediation Standards (RIDSRS), and the Residential Inhalation Exposure Pathway Soil Remediation  Standards (RISRS).  TASK 5   –   CONCRETE LETTER REPORT  Upon completion of the concrete sampling activities, Matrix will summarize the   concrete data in table format  and the results will be compared to the NJDEP’s soil remediation standards .   The letter report will include a  site plan depicting the sample locations, analytical summary tables, figures with analytical results summary,  and preliminary recommendations .   Matrix can also provide an associated cost estimate and estimated timeline  for beneficial reuse evaluation or disposal of the concrete , at additional cost .  TASK   6   –   CONTRACT DOCUMENTS AND BID SUPPORT  Matrix will complete a site   in spection   and prepare project specifications for the building removal.   Matrix   will  coordinate with all utility companies, as required, regarding the shutoff of the existing utilities .  It is anticipated that local Soil Conservation District approval will be required for this project. Matrix will prepare  the application forms and plans for certification as part of this task; however, this proposal does not include  any application fees. Th e project specifications will include information on soil erosion requirements. The final  site will include topsoil and seed restoration .  The specifications will be prepared in accordance with the public bidding format. Once specifications are  complete Matrix will coordinate with the Township and provide assistance throughout the bidding process.  This will include the advertisement of the bi d, response to all bidder questions and the review of the bids, and  preparation of a recommendation of award to the Township.  T ASK   7   –   PROJECT MANAGEMENT  This task includes project management and   in   anticipation of future conference calls and/or meetings with the  project team. As a part of this task, the Matrix LSRP will oversee the activities outlined above.  COSTS  All work will be completed in accordance with our terms and conditions (attached). 1  106 - 25  March 25, 2025  RESOLU TION   ADJUSTING RATES FOR EXTRA DUTY SERVICES RELATED TO  WEST ORANGE POLICE DEPARTMENT  WHEREAS ,   on March 25, 2025, the Township Council for the Township of West  Orange (the “Township”) adopted Ordinance 2902 - 25 (the “Ordinance”); and  WHEREAS ,   the   Ordinance requires that the fees for extra duty assignments for police  officers will be set by Resolution ; and  WHEREAS ,   Resolution 298 - 23 sets forth the fees and/or fines associated with various  ordinances in the Township of West Orange Municipal Code; and  WHE REAS,   this Resolution shall override Resolution 298 - 23 with respect to   the fees set  forth   Chapter 4, Section 34.3,   only ;   and  NOW, BEING HEREBY RESOLVED BY THE TOWNSHIP COUNCIL OF THE  TOWNSHIP OF WEST ORANGE,   that   the fees associated with the Ordinance shall be as  follows:  Section of  Ordinance/Township Code  Administration Fee   per  Hour  Hourly Rate  §   34.3(a)(1)   (Steady  Security/Crowd   Control  Assignments)  $4   $80  §   34.3(a)(2)   (Special   Event  Security   and   Holiday/Crowd  Control Assignments)  $4   $80  §   34.3(a)(4)(a)   (Weekday  traffic/road construction)  $15   $100  §   34.3(a)(4)(b)   (weekend  traffic/road construction)  $15   $115  §   34.3(a)(4)(c)   (road  construction requiring use of  vehicle)  $15   and   $25   per   hour   if   a  police vehicle is needed  $100  §   34.3(a)(4)(d)   (emergency  road work assignments)  $15   and   $25   per   hour   if   a  police vehicle is needed  $115

2902 - 25  AN ORDINANCE AMENDING CHAPTER   4 , SECTION   34 . 3   OF THE REVISED GENERAL  ORDINANCES OF THE TOWNSHIP OF WEST ORANGE  ( EXTRA DUTY EMPLOYMENT FOR POLICE OFFICERS - RATES OF COMPENSATION;  ADMINISTRATIVE FEES; PAYMENT FOR SERVICES )  BE IT ORDAINED BY THE MUNICIPAL COUNCIL OF THE TOWNSHIP OF   WEST  ORANGE, NEW JERSEY   that Chapter   4 , Section   34 . 3   o f the Revised General   Ordinances of the  Township of West Orange be and are hereby amended as follows:  I.   PURPOSE  The purpose of this ordinance is   to   modify the   fines   set forth in Chapter   4 , Section   34.3   of the  Revised General Ordinances of the Township of West Orange to be amendable by Resolution   such that   it  is   amendable in the future by approval   of the Township Council, a necessity in   an everchanging  socioeconomic climate .  II.   CHAPTER   4 ,   SECTION   34.3   SHALL   BE   AND   HEREBY   IS   AMENDED   AND  SUPPLEMENTED   TO INCORPORATE THE FOLLOWING:  § 4 - 34.3   BE AND HEREBY AMENDED AS FOLLOWS:  •   The portion entitled “ Rates of Compensation; Administrative Fees; Payment for Services ” which  specifies   the rate of compensation to employees of the Township Police Department performing  Extra Duty Services   shall be repealed and replaced by the following language:  Because of the myriad types of assignments that Police Officers may be called upon to  perform, it is reasonable that different rates apply.  a.   Unless there are contractual agreements with a vendor/contractor to the contrary, the  following rates of compensation apply:  1.   Steady Security/Crowd Control Assignments. Steady security and crowd control  assignments shall be paid at a rate   as prescribed by Resolution.   This rate includes a n  administrative fee prescribed by the same Resolution.  2.   Special Event   Security   and   Holiday /Crowd Control   Assignments. Special event  security   and   crowd   control   assignments   shall   be   paid   at   a   rate   as   prescribed   by  Resolution .   This rate includes an administrative fee prescribed by the same Resolution .  Special event security/crowd control assignments are defined as assignments not worked  on a regular/steady basis.  4.   Traffic/Road Construction. Assignments for all traffic control at construction site  assignments shall be as follows:  (a)   Assignments between 12:00 a.m. on Monday and 11:59 p.m. on Friday shall  be paid at a rate   as prescribed by Resolution. This rate includes an administrative  fee prescribed by the same Resolution.  (b)   Assignments between 12:00 a.m. on Saturday and 11:59 p.m. on Sunday shall  be paid at a rate   as prescribed by Resolution. This rate includes an administrative  fee prescribed by the same   Resolution.  (c)   All road construction assignments that require the use of a marked patrol unit  shall be charged   additionally, as prescribed by Resolution . This is in addition to  the administrative fees already billed. The assignment of the vehicle will be  Deleted:   3.   Special Event - New Year's Eve. Special event -  New Year's Eve assignments shall be paid at a rate   as  prescribed by Resolution. This rate includes an  administrative fee to be prescribed by the same Resolution. ¶

2  4848 - 4827 - 0498, v. 1  determined by the Police Administration and availability of the vehicles at the  time of the request.  (d) All emergency road work assignments   which are received by the Township  on less than 24   hours’ notice   shall require a separate fee paid at a rate as  prescribed by Resolution. This rate includes an administrative fee prescrib ed by  the same Resolution.  b.   All assignments shall have a   four - hour   minimum per officer contracted.  c.   Officers are entitled to the minimum hours of compensation stipulated in subsection 4 -  34.3b if their assignment is cancelled upon their arrival at the job site without advance  notice stipulated in subsection 4 - 34.4c.  d.   Officers who begin an assignment, but who are cancelled prior to the completion of the  assignment shall be entitled to the minimum compensation stipulated in subsection 4 -  34.3b or the number of hours worked, whichever is longer.  III.   REPEAL OF CONFLICTING ORDINANCES  Any Ordinances of the Township which are in conflict with this Ordinance are hereby repealed to  the extent of such conflict.  IV .   SEVERABILITY  If any part of this Ordinance shall be deemed invalid, such parts shall be severed and the  invalidity thereof shall not affect the remaining parts of this Ordinance.  V .   EFFECTIVE DATE  This Ordinance shall take effect upon final passage and publication in accordance with the law.  SUSAN MCCARTNEY , MAYOR  JOE KRAKOVIAK  COUNCIL PRESIDENT  KAREN J. CARNEVALE, R.M.C.  MUNICIPAL CLERK  INTRODUCED:   March 11, 2025  ADOPTED:   March 25, 2025

3  4848 - 4827 - 0498, v. 1  2902 - 25  Legislative History  The purpose of this ordinance is   to modify the f ees   set forth in Chapter   4   of the  Revised General Ordinances of the Township of West Orange to be subject to adjustment by  Resolution   and revise the categories of assignments and simply same .

1  2905-25  AN ORDINANCE AMENDING   AND SUPPLEMENTING   CHAPTER   28 AND  ESTABLISHING   SECTION S   OF THE REVISED GENERAL ORDINANCES OF THE  TOWNSHIP OF WEST ORANGE   REGARDING A LAWN CARE REGISTRY  BE   IT   ORDAINED   BY   THE   MUNICIPAL   COUNCIL   OF   THE   TOWNSHIP   OF  WEST   ORANGE,   NEW   JERSEY   that   Chapter   28   of   the   Revised   General   Ordinances   of   the  Township of West Orange be and   are   hereby   supplemented   as follows:  I.   PURPOSE  The   purpose   of   this   ordinance   is   to   establish   a   lawn   care   registry   for   businesses   operating  a commercial lawn business as defined herein.  § 28-17— Definitions  Commercial   Lawn   Maintenance   Busines s   -   A   business   that   performs   lawn   and   yard  maintenance   within   the   Township   where   the   cutting   of   grass   and   removal   of   leaves   are  performed .  § 28-18— Registration of Commercial Law n   Maintenance Businesses  A.   All   commercial   lawn   maintenance   businesses   must   register   with   the   Township   Public  Works   Department   by   March   1   of   each   year,   unless   the   business   is   registered   with   the  State   of   New   Jersey   under   the   Contractors'   Registration   Act   (N.J.S.A.   56:8-136   et   seq.)  and   New   Jersey   Administrative   Code   §   13:45A-17.2.   For   2025   only,   the   registration  deadline   will   be   July   1,   2025 .   Businesses   that   are   already   registered   with   the   State   of  New   Jersey   must   submit   a   copy   of   their   state   registration   to   the   Township   Public   Works  Department   by   March   1   each   year.   Registered   lawn   care   companies   will   be   listed   on   the  Township website.  B.   The   following   information   must   be   provided   to   the   Township   during   the   registration  process:  1.   Name(s) of the business owner(s)  2.   Address of the business owner(s) and the business  3.   Telephone number(s) of the business owner(s)  4.   Email address(es) of the business owner(s)  5.   License plate number(s) of all business vehicles (trucks)  C.   There   is   no   registration   fee   for   this   process.   Each   registered   business   vehicle   (truck)  will   be   issued   a   sticker   by   the   Township,   which   must   be   affixed   to   the   front   driver ’ s   side  panel of the vehicle.  §28-19— Enforcement.

2  This   chapter   shall   be   enforced   by   the   Township   Police   Department,   Public   Works   Department   or  other Township   employees   as designated by the Township Business Administrator.  §28-20— Violations and penalties.  A.   Any   person   found   to   be   in   violation   of   the   provisions   of   this   chapter   shall   be   subject   to   a   fine  as follows:  (1) First offense: $100.  (2) Second offense: $200.  (3) Any subsequent offense: an amount not to exceed $500.  B. Each day a violation continues shall be deemed a separate offense.  I I .   REPEAL OF CONFLICTING ORDINANCES  Any   Ordinances   of   the   Township   which   conflict   with   this   Ordinance   are   hereby   repealed  to the extent of such conflict.  I II .   SEVERABILITY  If   any   part   of   this   Ordinance   shall   be   deemed   invalid,   such   determination   shall   only   apply  to   those   specific   section(s),   paragraph(s),   subparagraph(s),   clause(s)   or   provision(s)   and   those  parts   shall   be   severed .   T h e   invalidity   thereof   shall   not   a ffect   the   remaining   parts   of   this  Ordinance   which shall be deemed valid and   effective.  SUSAN McCARTNEY, MAYOR  JOE KRAKOVIAK  COUNCIL PRESIDENT  KAREN J. CARNEVALE, R.M.C.,  MUNICIPAL CLERK  INTRODUCED:   March 25, 2025  ADOPTED:   April 8, 2025

3  2905-25  Legislative History  The purpose of this Ordinance is to establish a registry for commercial lawn care  businesses as defined herein.  VOTE SHEET  2905-25   AYE   NAY   ABSTAIN  3.25.25   FIRST RDG.  MOVED BY  SECOND  Casalino  Ghebremicael  Rudin  Scarpa  Krakoviak

1  2906 - 25  AN ORDNANCE AMENDING CHAPTER 10 TO ADD A NEW SECTION OF THE  REVISED GENERAL ORDINANCES OF THE TOWNSHIP OF WEST ORANGE  REGARDING SINGLE - USE BATHROOMS  BE IT ORDANED BY THE MUNICIPAL COUNCIL OF THE TOWNSHIP OF  WEST ORANGE, NEW JERSEY   that Chapter 10 of the Revised General Ordinances of the  Township of West Orange be and are hereby amended as follows:  I.   PURPOSE  The purpose of this Ordinance is to require any business establishment to designate all  single - occupancy bathrooms as gender neutral.  § 10 - _____ : Definitions  Single - Occupancy Facility or Single - Occupancy Restroom : Shall mean a toilet facility with  one toilet in the facility, or a toilet and one urinal without a privacy stall separating the two with  a locking mechanism controlled by the user on the door to enter the facility.  § 10 - ____: Requirements for Single - Occupancy Restrooms  A.   All   single - occupancy   restrooms   in   any   business   establishment,   place   of   public  accommodation, and Township - owned building or facility shall be designated as all  gender for   any individual, family, or assisted use. This   includes single - occupancy  restrooms intended for use by Township employees or employees of a business or public  accommodation.  B.   Existing   business   establishments,   places   of   public   accommodation,   and   Township  facilities subject to this section shall have 60 days from the effective date of this  Ordinance to comply by replacing existing gender - specific signage on the exterior of  single - occupancy restrooms with signs that do not indicate a specific gender (such as  signs that read "restroom"). New businesses, places of public accommodation, and  Township facilities shall be in compliance with this section immediately upon opening to  the public.  C.   During any inspection of a business or a place of public accommodation by an employee  of the Township of West Orange Zoning Department, Building Depar t ment, or other local  official, the inspector or official may inspect for compliance with this Ordinance.

2  II.   REPEAL OF CONFLICTING ORDINANCES  Any Ordinances of the Township which are in conflict with this Ordinance are hereby  repealed to the extent of such conflict.  III.   SEVERABILITY  If any part of this Ordinance shall be deemed invalid, such determination shall only apply  to those specific section(s), paragraph(s), subparagraph(s), clause(s) or provision(s) and those  parts shall be severed. The invalidity thereof shall not affect the remaining parts of this  Ordinance which shall be deemed valid and effective.  IV.   EFFECTIVE DATE  This Ordinance shall take effect upon final passage and publication in accordance with  the law  SUSAN McCARTNEY, MAYOR  JOE KRAKOVIAK  COUNCIL PRESIDENT  KAREN J. CARNEVALE, R.M.C.,  MUNICIPAL CLERK  INTRODUCED:   March 25, 2025  ADOPTED:   April 8, 2025

1  2907-25  AN ORDINANCE AMENDING   AND SUPPLEMENTING   CHAPTER   2,   SECTION   ___  OF THE REVISED GENERAL ORDINANCES OF THE TOWNSHIP OF WEST  ORANGE   TO ESTABLISH A CITIZENS BUDGET ADVISORY BOARD  BE   IT   ORDAINED   BY   THE   MUNICIPAL   COUNCIL   OF   THE   TOWNSHIP   OF  WEST   ORANGE,   NEW   JERSEY   that   Chapter   2   of   the   Revised   General   Ordinances   of   the  Township of West Orange be and   are   hereby amended as follows:  I.   PURPOSE  The   purpose   of   this   Ordinance   is   to   establish   a   Citizens   Budget   Advisory   Board   via  Ordinance .  § 2-____: Definitions  Citizens   Budget   Advisory   Board:   An   advisory   board   made   up   of   members   of   the   public  appointed   by   both   the   Township   Council   and   the   Mayor   charged   with   reviewing   the   Township ’ s  Budget,   its   services,   programs   and   expenditures,   compiling   the   Board ’ s   collective   analysis,   input  and   suggestions   and   presenting   them   to   the   Township   Council   by   regular   reports   and  presentations, as appropriate .  § 2-____: Establishment of a   Citizens Advisory Board  A.   The   Citizens   Budget   Advisory   Board   shall   be   comprised   of:   (i)   one   member  appointed   by   the   Mayor;   (ii)   o ne   member   appointed   by   each   of   the   five   members   of  the   Township   Council;   and   (iii)   one   consensus   candidate   chosen   by   the   majority   of  the Council.  B.   Candidates   for   the   board   shall   be   Township   residents,   work   in   the   Township,  employed   by   a   company   based   in   the   Township,   or   own   or   lease   property   in   the  Township.   The   chair   shall   be   selected   by   a   majority   vote   of   the   members,   as   promptly  as   practicable   once   appointments   are   made.   The   term   of   each   Board   member   will   be  one   calendar   year,   starting   on   January   1   of   the   year   that   the   members   serve,   and  members   may   be   reappointed   in   subsequent   years.   Appointments   shall   be   made  before   January   1   of   the   year   that   me mbers   serve.   In   the   case   of   any   newly   elected  Councilmember,   his/her   appointment,   as   well   as   the   appointment   of   the   Council  consensus, should be made as soon as practicable once he/she takes the oath of office.  C.   A   Board   member   can   resign   by   sending   a   letter   or   email   to   the   Municipal   Clerk   which  shall   take   effect   immediately.   Any   Board   member   may   be   removed   with   or   without  cause, at any time, by the person appointing the Board member.  D.   Board   members   are   required   to   attend   all   scheduled   Board   meetings   and   strongly  encouraged   to   attend   all   Council   meetings   that   focus   on   the   budget,   including   review  of   any   video   or   audio   recordings   of   such   meetings.   Failure   to   attend   three   Board

2  meetings   may   be   grounds   for   removal   from   the   Board   by   a   written   motion   to   dismiss  that   can   be   offered   by   any   Board   member.   The   Board   member   at   issue   may   request   a  public   hearing   of   the   cause(s)   and   present   a   defense.   That   hearing   shall   be   followed  by   deliberation   in   open   session.   A   majority   vote   decides   the   issue,   with   a   tie   vote  allowing the Board member to continue to serve.  E.   The Board shall:  a.   Make   all   requests   for   information   or   materials   following   a   majority   vote   of   the  members present.  b.   Have   access   to   all   financial   and   budget   documents   that   it   believes   are   needed  to   advise   the   Council   that   are   not   otherwise   subject   to   privacy   or   other   non-  disclosure   limitations.   The   Board   shall   make   its   request   in   writing   to   the  Council,   which   will   review   the   request   and   forward   it   to   the   Business  Administrator or his/her designee, and the Chief Financial Officer.  c.   Request   in   writing   through   the   Council   to   the   Business   Administrator   or  his/her   designee,   and   the   Chief   Financial   Officer   to   meet   with   the   Chief  Financial   Officer   or   others   as   appropriate   for   presentations   and   discussions   on  these financial issues outside of a regular or special Council meeting.  d.   Take   m inutes   of   meetings   and   submit   approved   Minutes   to   the   Council,  Business   Administrator   and   Chief   Financial   Officer.   The   Board   will   also  provide   the   m inutes   to   the   Municipal   Clerk   for   public   availability   in   the  Clerk ’ s office and for posting to the Township website .  e.   Submit   written   reports   to   the   Council,   Business   Administrator   and   Chief  Financial   Officer   on   a   regular   basis   of   the   Board ’ s   findings,   questions   and  recommendations.  f.   Appear   as   requested   by   the   Town   Council   at   Council   meetings   to   present  and/or   engage   in   discussions   with   Councilmembers   and   Administration  personnel, as appropriate.  g.   Meet   at   least   once   a   month   in   the   year   that   members   serve,   because   Budget  issues   are   a   year-round   responsibility,   including   year-end   account  reconciliations   and   pre-planning   for   the   following   calendar   year.   Meetings  may occur more frequently depending on the majority vote of members.

3  h.   Communicate   as   appropriate   with   the   Councilmember   or   Mayor   who  appointed   them   on   budget   matters.   Members   can   also   communicate   through  the   Board   Chair   or   his/her   designee   with   county   or   state   agencies   as  appropriate,   as   well   as   other   municipalities   and   other   entities   that   may   perform  similar   advisory   duties   in   other   municipalities.   When   communicating,   the  Chair   or   designee   shall   clearly   describe   his   or   her   role   as   a   member   of   the  West   Orange   Citizens   Budget   Advisory   Board,   a   volunteer   body   created   by  Ordinan ce   advising   the   Township   Council   to   develop   the   annual   budget   and  related financial areas.  I I .   REPEAL OF CONFLICTING ORDINANCES  Any   Ordinances   of   the   Township   which   are   in   conflict   with   this   Ordinance   are   hereby  repealed to the extent of such conflict.  I II .   SEVERABILITY  If   any   part   of   this   Ordinance   shall   be   deemed   invalid,   such   determination   shall   only   apply  to   those   specific   section(s),   paragraph(s),   subparagraph(s),   clause(s)   or   provision(s)   and   those  parts   shall   be   severed .   T h e   invalidity   thereof   shall   not   a ffect   the   remaining   parts   of   this  Ordinance   which shall be deemed valid and effective.  IX .   EFFECTIVE DATE  This   Ordinance   shall   take   effect   upon   final   passage   and   publication   in   accordance   with  the law.  SUSAN McCARTNEY, MAYOR  JOE   KRAKOVIAK  COUNCIL PRESIDENT  KAREN J. CARNEVALE, R.M.C.,  MUNICIPAL CLERK  INTRODUCED:   March 25, 2025  ADOPTED:   April 8,   2025

4  7907-25  Legislative History  This   Ordinance   will   establish   the   creation   of   a   Citizens   Board   Advisory   Board   (the  “ Board ” )   by   Ordinance.   In   years   past,   the   Township   previously   had   the   Board   by   Resolution.  The purpose of this Ordinance is to codify the rules and regulations of the Board via Ordinance. 2908 - 25  AN ORDINANCE AMENDING CHAPTER   2 , S E CTION   9. 4   GENERAL ORDINANCES  OF THE TOWNSHIP OF WEST ORANGE  ( SPECIAL COUNSEL )  BE IT ORDAINED BY THE MUNICIPAL COUNCIL OF THE TOWNSHIP OF  WEST ORANGE, NEW JERSEY   that Chapter   2, Section s   9. 4   o f   the Revised General   Ordinances  of the Township of West Orange be and   are   hereby amended as follows:  I.   PURPOSE  The purpose of this ordinance is   to   modify the   provisions of the Township of West Orange  Municipal Code governing the Township’s retention of Special Counsel .   This ordinance: (i)  requires appointment of Special Counsel by advice and consent of the Township Council; and (ii)  require that the bills for Special Counsel shall be subject to approval from the Township Council.  I I .   CHAPTER   2 SECTION S   9.4   SHALL BE AND HEREBY IS AMENDED AND  SUPPLEMENTED   TO INCORPORATE THE FOLLOWING:  2 - 9. 4   Special Counsel .  Subject to   the advise   and consent of the   Township Council , the Township Attorney may appoint  such Special Counsel as may be required or advisable to handle matters in which a conflict of  interest precludes representation by the Township Attorney or Assistant Township Attorney, or  which due to its complexity, spe cial nature, time demands or constraints, or for any good reason  would benefit from the special experience and/or training of the Special Counsel to be hired. Such  Special Counsel need not serve full time and would be expected to serve for the duration of   the  matter for which he or she was appointed, subject to annual reappointment with the manner and  amount of compensation to be approved by the   Township   Coun cil . Special Counsel would be  subject to the supervision of the Township Attorney   except for matters   involving a conflict of  interest precluding representation by the Township Attorney wherein   Special Counsel would be  subject to the supervision of the   Assistant Township Attorney   or designated conflict counsel .   T he  bills of all Special Counsel   shall be subject to approval by the   Township Council. The Township  Council must receive written notification from the Administration detailing the reasons for  engaging Special Counsel,   including the name of the law firm to be engaged, the rate, and a “not  to exceed” clause.  III.   REPEAL OF CONFLICTING ORDINANCES  Any Ordinances of the Township which are in conflict with   this Ordinance are hereby  repealed to the extent of such conflict.  IV .   SEVERABILITY  If any part of this Ordinance shall be deemed invalid, such parts shall be severed and th e  invalidity thereof shall not a ffect the remaining parts of this   Ordinance. 2  V .   EFFECTIVE DATE  This Ordinance shall take effect upon final passage and publication in accordance with the  law.  SUSAN MCCARTNEY , MAYOR  JOSEPH KRAKOVIAK ,  COUNCIL PRESIDENT  KAREN J. 2909 - 25  LEGISLATIVE HISTORY  This Ordinance is in response to requests from handicapped residents who reside at 26 Babcock  Place, 268 Watchung Avenue, and 39 Washington Street.  By adopting this Ordinance, the Township will establish a handicapped parking stall adjacent to  each of these addresses. In the case of 39 Washington Street, the stall will be placed on the side of the  home on High Street, because the resident’s access if from that side. Such parking stalls are permissible  under the laws of New Jersey, and they are designated as Restricted Parking Zones. All other Parking  Ordinances under this chapter apply and supersede the use of these restricted spaces.  Additionally, two Handicapped Stalls located in Municipal Plaza nearest the Police Building will  get limits of 1 hour parking for the convenience of residents that have business there. Two other stalls  will be designated Handicapped for use of employees or others that need to park all day.

This Section 4 shall not  apply, however, with respect to any contribution or grant in aid received by the Township as  a result of using funds from this bond ordinance as “matching local funds” to receive such  contribution or grant in aid.  SECTION 5.   All bond anticipation notes issued hereunder shall mature at  such times as may be determined by the Chief Financial Officer of the Township   (the “Chief  Financial Officer”), provided that no note shall mature later than one (1) year from its date.  The notes shall bear interest at such rate or rates and be in such form as may be  determined by the Chief Financial Officer.   The Chief Financial Officer shall determine all  matters in connection with the notes issued pursuant to this bond ordinance, and the  signature of the Chief Financial Officer upon the notes shall be conclusive evidence as to all  such determinations.   All notes issued hereunder may be renewed from time to time in  accordance with the provisions of N.J.S.A. § 40A:2 - 8.1 of the Local Bond Law.   The Chief  Financial Officer is hereby authorized to sell part or all of the notes from time to time at a  public or private sale and to deliver them to the purchaser thereof upon receipt of payment  of the purchase price and accrued interest thereon from their dates to the date of delivery  thereof.   The Chief Financial Officer is directed to report in writing to the governing body at  the meeting next succeeding the date when any sale or delivery of the notes pursuant to  this   bond   ordinance   is   made.   Such   report   must   include   the   principal   amount,   the

The capital budget or  temporary capital budget, as applicable, of the Township is hereby amended to conform  with the provisions of this bond ordinance to the extent of any inconsistency herewith. SECTION 7.   The following additional matters   are hereby   determined,  declared, recited and stated:  (a)   The improvements and purposes described in Section 3 of this  bond ordinance are not current expenses and are improvements and purposes which the  Township may lawfully undertake as general improvements and no part of the cost thereof  has been or shall be specially assessed on property specially benefited thereby.  (b)   The   average   period   of   usefulness   of   said   improvements   or  purposes, computed on the basis of the respective amounts of obligations authorized for  each purpose and the reasonable life thereof in accordance with the limitations set forth in  the Local Bond Law, is   11.10   years.  (c)   The supplemental debt statement required by the Local Bond  Law has been duly made and filed in the Office of the Clerk of the Township and a complete  executed duplicate thereof has been filed in the Office of the Director of the Division of  Local Government Services in the Department of Community Affairs of the State of New  Jersey, and such statement shows that the gross debt of the Township as defined in the

7  #95178364.1 168182.012  Local Bond Law is increased by the authorization of the bonds or notes provided for in this  bond ordinance by $ 9,387, 109 .   The obligations authorized by this bond ordinance will be  within all debt limitations prescribed by said Local Bond Law.  (d)   An amount not exceeding $ 1,000,000   for items of expense listed  in and permitted under N.J.S.A. § 40A:2 - 20 of the Local Bond Law is included in the  estimated cost indicated herein for the purposes and improvements described in Section 3  hereof.  SECTION 8.   The full faith and credit of the Township are hereby pledged  to the punctual payment of the principal of and the interest on the obligations authorized by  this bond ordinance.   The obligations shall be direct, unlimited obligations of the Township  and, unless paid from other sources, the Township shall be obligated to levy   ad valorem  taxes upon all the taxable property within the Township for the payment of the principal of  the obligations and the interest thereon without limitation as to rate or amount.  SECTION 9.   The   Township   reasonably   expects   to   reimburse   any  expenditures toward the costs of the improvements or purposes described in Section 3  hereof and paid prior to the issuance of any bonds or notes authorized by this bond  ordinance with the proceeds of such bonds or notes.   This Section 9 is intended to be and  hereby is a declaration of the Township’s official intent to reimburse any expenditure toward  the costs of the improvements or purposes described in Section 3 hereof to be incurred and  paid prior to the issuance of bonds or notes authorized herein in accordance with Treasu ry  Regulation Section 1.150 - 2.  SECTION 10.   The Chief Financial Officer is hereby authorized to prepare  and to update from time to time as necessary a financial disclosure document to be  distributed in connection with the sale of the obligations of the Township authorized herein

8  #95178364.1 168182.012  and to execute such disclosure document on behalf of the Township.   The Chief Financial  Officer is further authorized to enter into the appropriate undertaking to provide secondary  market disclosure on behalf of the Township pursuant to Rule 15c2 - 12 of the Securities and  Exchange Commission (the "Rule") for the benefit of holders and beneficial owners of the  obligations of the Township, which are authorized herein, and to amend such undertaking  from time to time in connection with any change in law, or interpretation thereof, provided  such undertaking is and continues to be, in the opinion of a nationally recognized bond  counsel, consistent with the requirements of the Rule.   In the event that the Township fails  to comply with its undertaking, the Township shall not be liable for any monetary damages,  and the remedy shall be limited to specific performance of the undertaking. The Township covenants to maintain the exclusion from  gross income under Section 103(a) of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended, of  the interest on all bonds and notes issued on a tax - exempt basis under this bond ordinance. #95178364.1 168182.012  TOWNSHIP COUNCIL OF THE TOWNSHIP OF WEST ORANGE  IN THE COUNTY OF ESSEX, NEW JERSEY  PUBLIC NOTICE  NOTICE OF PENDING BOND ORDINANCE AND SUMMARY  The bond ordinance, the summary terms of which are included herein, was introduced and passed upon first  reading at a meeting of the Township Council of the Township of West Orange, in the County of Essex, State of New  Jersey (the “Township”) on   March 25, 2025 .   It will be further considered for final passage, after public hearing  thereon, at a meeting of the governing body of the Township to be held at the Township Municipal Building, 66 Main  Street, West Orange, New Jersey, in said County, on   April   8 ,   2025   at 7:00 p.m.   During the week prior to and up to  and including the date of such meeting, copies of the full bond ordinance will be available at no cost and during  regular business hours at the Clerk’s office for the members of the general public who shall request the same.   The  summary of the terms of such bond ordinance follows:  Title:   BOND ORDINANCE PROVIDING FOR VARIOUS 202 5   CAPITAL   ACQUISITIONS AND  IMPROVEMENTS, BY AND IN THE TOWNSHIP OF WEST ORANGE, IN THE COUNTY  OF ESSEX, STATE OF NEW JERSEY, APPROPRIATING $ 12,835,000   THEREFOR AND  AUTHORIZING THE ISSUANCE OF $ 9,387,109   BONDS OR NOTES OF THE TOWNSHIP  TO FINANCE PART OF THE COST THEREOF  Purpose(s):   The bond ordinance   provides for (i)   improvements and repairs to   various roads   in the  Township ;   ( i i)   improvements and repairs to Lando Parking Lot; ( iii )   purchase of signs striping  and LED lights   along Kelly Drive ;   ( iv )   improvements and repairs to Dogwood Drive at Boland  Park ;   and   ( v )   exterior improvements and building and grounds improvements   along Rooney  Circle .   Please   refer to the full bond ordinance for a more complete description of the  improvements and purposes of the bond ordinance. #95178331.1 168182.012  TOWNSHIP   COUNCIL OF THE   TOWNSHIP   OF   WEST ORANGE  PUBLIC NOTICE  NOTICE OF INTRODUCTION OF CAPITAL ORDINANCE  The capital ordinance, published herewith in its   entirety , has been introduced  by the   Township   Council of the   Township   of   West Orange , in the County of   Essex , State of  New Jersey (the “ Township ”) on   March 25, 2025 .  The purpose of this capital ordinance is to appropriate   $ 1,000,000   received  or expected to be received from a grant provided by the   New Jersey Department of  Community   Affairs   –   Coronavirus   State   Fiscal   Recovery   Fund   to   pay   for   various  improvements   and upgrades to   (i)   the Oskar Schindler Performing Arts Center   including,  but not limited to,   the installation of a sound system for the amphitheater,   a shed,   the  installation of ADA   accessible seating   ,   and the installation of safety lighting   on the  building,   stage flooring ,   and   security cameras   (ii) Crystal Lake, including, but not limited to,  the   construction of   two floating docks   .   S aid   improvement s   or purpose s   shall also  include, as applicable, all work, materials, equipment, engineering, design, consulting  work, preparation of plans and specifications, permits, bid documents, conducting and  preparation   of   reports   and   studies,   environmental   investigations   and   remediation,  equipment rental, labor and appurtenances necessary therefore or incidental thereto.  This capital ordinance will be considered for final adoption by the   Township  Council of the   Township   after public hearing thereon, at a meeting of the governing body  taking place at   the   Township   Municipal Building,   66 Main Street ,   West Orange,   New  Jersey ,   to be held on   April   8 ,   2025   at 7: 00   p.m.   A copy of this ordinance ma y be  obtained without cost from   the Office of the   Township   Clerk,   66 Main Street, West Orange ,  New Jersey , during regular business hours .  ___________________________________  KAREN J.
